target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw runtime (Node 22+)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `canvasHost.activity.enabled=true`, `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=true`, Discord account configured

### Steps

1. Configure Discord Activity URL mapping and OpenClaw activity settings.
2. Launch via Discord `launch-activity` button and observe canvas/auth behavior.
3. Start Discord provider and verify native command deploy behavior when Entry Point command exists.

### Expected

- Activity launches to canvas route and passes intended auth gates.
- Native command deploy retains Entry Point command and does not fail with 50240.

### Actual

- Verified in updated tests and runtime flow for this branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran targeted tests covering deploy flow, gateway auth flow, prompt/runtime guidance, and canvas helper injection.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Default-on launch-context enforcement and explicit opt-out behavior.
  - Entry Point command retention path and 50240 handling path.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Discord OAuth backend token-exchange endpoint flow (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Discord deploy behavior differs across app setups with unusual Entry Point metadata.
  - Mitigation: Entry Point payload is sourced from Discord's existing command record and included only when missing from outgoing bulk payload.
- Risk: Launch-context default-on may block intentionally direct/manual canvas opens for activity mode.
  - Mitigation: Explicit opt-out via `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=false`.

target.title: fix(plugins): iterate all providers in wrapProviderStreamFn
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `wrapProviderStreamFn()` only checked a single provider via `resolveProviderHookPlugin()`. When a built-in provider (e.g. Anthropic) owned the provider ID, external plugins with matching `hookAliases` could never contribute their own `wrapStreamFn`.
- **Why it matters:** External plugins that want to compose stream transformations (e.g. injecting custom tool types into the API payload) on top of a built-in provider's wrapper are silently ignored.
- **What changed:** Applied the same iteration pattern already used by `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin()` and `normalizeProviderConfigWithPlugin()`: check the primary matched plugin first, then iterate all providers from `resolveProviderPluginsForHooks()`.
- **Scope:** Only `wrapProviderStreamFn()` in `src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts`. No other functions changed.

## Change Type

Bug fix — `wrapStreamFn` on external plugins with `hookAliases` is accepted at registration but never called when a built-in provider matches.

## Root Cause

`wrapProviderStreamFn()` called `resolveProviderHookPlugin()` which returns the first matching provider (typically the built-in). The built-in provider's `wrapStreamFn` was used exclusively. External plugins registered via `hookAliases` were discovered by `resolveProviderPluginsForHooks()` but never consulted for `wrapStreamFn`.

The pattern for iterating all providers already existed in `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin()` (line 457) and `normalizeProviderConfigWithPlugin()` (line 486) — `wrapProviderStreamFn` was the only provider hook that didn't follow it.

## Regression Test Plan

Added test: "chains wrapStreamFn from primary provider and hookAlias plugin"

- Registers two providers: a primary (`id: "anthropic"`) and an external plugin (`id: "custom-anthropic"`, `hookAliases: ["anthropic"]`)
- Both have `wrapStreamFn`
- Verifies the external plugin receives the primary's wrapped stream as `ctx.streamFn`
- Verifies the final result is the external plugin's wrapped function

Run: `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts`

## Security Impact

- **Auth/credentials:** No — does not touch auth flows
- **File system:** No
- **Network:** No — stream wrapping is in-process function composition
- **User data:** No

## Human Verification

- Confirmed test passes locally (18/18 in `provider-runtime.test.ts`)
- Confirmed the pattern matches `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin` exactly
- Confirmed chaining semantics: each subsequent plugin gets the previous wrapper's output as `ctx.streamFn`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(gateway): restore codex-cli chat history imports
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `chat.history` only backfilled external CLI transcript history for `claude-cli`, so Codex-backed sessions could look empty
after refresh when the local transcript was missing.
- Why it matters: users could lose visible history in Gateway/Control UI refresh flows even though the Codex CLI transcript still
existed on disk.

target.title: Test/run state machine clamp guard
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The `activeRuns` clamp guard in `onRunEnd()` (`Math.max(0, activeRuns - 1)`) was completely untested. Calling `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()` (e.g. error handling paths, race conditions) would cause `activeRuns` to go negative without this guard.
- Why it matters: A negative `activeRuns` would cause the machine to report incorrect state permanently, potentially breaking busy indicators across all channels.
- What changed: Added two unit tests covering the clamp guard — one for calling `onRunEnd()` with no prior `onRunStart()`, and one for calling it more times than `onRunStart()`.
- What did NOT change: No production code was changed. This is a test-only PR covering an existing defensive guard.

## Change Type (select all)

## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: The `Math.max(0, activeRuns - 1)` guard in `onRunEnd()` was added defensively but never had test coverage to verify it works correctly.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No unit test for mismatched `onRunStart`/`onRunEnd` calls.
- Contributing context (if known): Error handling paths or race conditions can trigger `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/channels/run-state-machine.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `activeRuns` never goes below zero regardless of call order.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Pure function, no dependencies, directly tests the guard condition.

- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — two tests added.

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
[onRunEnd x2] -> activeRuns = -1 (without guard, would break busy state)

After:
[onRunEnd x2] -> activeRuns = 0 (guard clamps correctly, now verified by test)
```

target.title: Fix/heartbeat task due clock skew
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `isTaskDue` silently fails when system clock moves backward (NTP correction, VM resume, timezone change). `nowMs - lastRunMs` becomes negative so the task never runs.
- Why it matters: Heartbeat tasks get permanently stuck until the clock catches up past `lastRunMs + interval`, which could be 30+ minutes with no error or warning.
- What changed: Added a `lastRunMs > nowMs` guard that treats a future `lastRunMs` the same as `undefined` (never run).
- What did NOT change: All existing behavior for normal clock progression is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: `isTaskDue` computes `nowMs - lastRunMs` without checking if `lastRunMs` is in the future. A backward clock jump makes this negative, so the condition `>= intervalMs` is never true.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No guard for `lastRunMs > nowMs` (clock skew scenario).
- Contributing context (if known): Can happen on any system after NTP sync, VM resume, or manual clock correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `isTaskDue` returns `true` when `lastRunMs > nowMs`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Pure function, no dependencies, single condition check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — `isTaskDue` had zero tests before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — test added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Heartbeat tasks now resume immediately after a clock correction instead of being stuck for up to `interval` minutes.

## Diagram (if applicable)
```text
Before:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs=future -> nowMs - lastRunMs < 0 -> task stuck forever

After:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs > nowMs -> treat as never-run -> task runs immediately
```

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A

### Steps
1. Set `lastRunMs` to a future timestamp (simulating clock skew)
2. Call `isTaskDue(futureMs, "30m", nowMs)`
3. Observe result

### Expected
- `true` (task should run)

### Actual (before fix)
- `false` (task permanently stuck)

## Evidence
- [x] Failing test before + passing after (new unit tests added)

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: clock skew (lastRunMs in future), normal elapsed interval, interval not yet elapsed, undefined lastRunMs
- Edge cases checked: lastRunMs exactly equal to nowMs (returns false, correct — not due yet)
- What you did NOT verify: actual NTP correction on live system

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: Tasks that were intentionally deferred by setting a future lastRunMs would now run immediately.
  - Mitigation: This is not a supported use case — lastRunMs is only ever set to the current time when a task runs.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: feat: expose model pricing to plugins via runtime.usage API
target.body:
## Summary
Adds a `runtime.usage` namespace to the plugin runtime, exposing `resolveModelCostConfig()` and `estimateUsageCost()` so context engine plugins can calculate model-cost estimates from OpenClaw's configured pricing instead of hardcoded heuristics.

## Motivation
Context engine plugins, such as [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), truncate stale or large context to save tokens. Today they can only estimate savings with a fixed heuristic. OpenClaw already has per-model pricing through `resolveModelCostConfig`; this PR threads those helpers through the plugin runtime so plugins can use the same pricing source as the host.

## Changes
- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts` — adds the runtime usage facade.
- `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` — adds the `usage` namespace to `PluginRuntimeCore`.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` — wires `createRuntimeUsage()` into runtime assembly.
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` — updates the plugin runtime mock.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts` — asserts the runtime exposes both usage helpers.
- `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md` — documents the plugin SDK usage helpers.
- `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` — regenerates the public SDK API baseline.

## Real Behavior Proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A plugin can access the new `api.runtime.usage` namespace and call both pricing helpers without importing OpenClaw internals.

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `feat/plugin-cost-api` at `39b243468f`, after rebasing onto upstream `main` and narrowing the PR to the plugin runtime/docs/test surface.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx .artifacts/pr64436-runtime-usage-proof/run-direct-plugin-api-proof.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from a minimal external plugin fixture with `openclaw.plugin.json` plus `index.mjs`; its `register(api)` calls `api.runtime.usage.resolveModelCostConfig(...)` and `api.runtime.usage.estimateUsageCost(...)`.

```text
[proof] repoHead=39b243468f
[proof] pluginDir=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im
[proof] manifest=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im/openclaw.plugin.json
[plugin] id=runtime-usage-proof
[plugin] hasRuntimeUsage=true
[plugin] resolveModelCostConfig cost={"input":3,"output":15,"cacheRead":0.3,"cacheWrite":3.75}
[plugin] estimateUsageCost total=0.010950
[proof] PASS plugin register(api) called both api.runtime.usage helpers
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The plugin saw `api.runtime.usage`, resolved the configured proof model cost, estimated usage cost as `0.010950`, and exited with the PASS line above.

- **What was not tested:** Full OpenClaw app startup and provider billing reconciliation; this proof is scoped to the plugin runtime API surface added by this PR.

## Local Verification
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `pnpm format:check docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.ts src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts -t 'exposes runtime.usage pricing helpers' --reporter=dot`

## Related
- Issue #64085 — Provider circuit breaker cost/status reporting.
- PR #64127 — companion status-provider work.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: feat: Rust/GTK4 Linux companion app (#75, alternative track)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a native Linux desktop companion under `apps/linux-gtk/` written in **Rust + GTK4 + libadwaita**
- Alternative-track contribution for [#75](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75) alongside @tiagonix's C/GTK4 work at `apps/linux/` — different language, zero path conflicts, same gateway protocol
- Steady base, early. Needs security review, packaging, and UI polish

## Why another one

I started on a prototype using web-tech (Electron) before reading through #75, then saw [@steipete's comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75#issuecomment-3747113141):

> My vision for this is a cute gtk-native Linux app. Esp for the statusbar features.

Switched tracks to native GTK. @tiagonix's C implementation was the reference, but I wanted to see what the Rust ecosystem gives us here (borrow-checked gateway client, fewer NULL pointer surprises, tokio for async RPC, `cargo test` for unit coverage).

Happy to fold it in, leave it as a community showcase, or archive it — whichever serves the project.

## What's included

**Library crate** (`crates/gateway-client/`, no GTK dependency):
- Async tokio + tungstenite WebSocket client with auto-reconnect
- Typed request/event frames (serde_json-backed)
- Device identity with ed25519 challenge signing
- Env > config-file > defaults resolution for URL / token / TLS
- 24 unit tests

**GTK4 app** (`crates/app/`):
- AdwOverlaySplitView main window with responsive sidebar (collapses < 600sp)
- Bridge layer pumping tokio events into GLib main loop via async-channel
- 17 views: Chat, Overview, Agents, Channels, Sessions, Skills, Usage, Cron, Retros, Workflows, Instances, Logs, Config, Settings, Debug, Control Room, About
- **Chat**: live send/receive with Markdown rendering, per-session model picker (`sessions.patch`), typing indicator, thinking/tool-call toggles, history via `chat.history`
- **Agents**: master/detail with editable settings (`agents.update`) and workspace files (`agents.files.list`)
- Snapshot refresh pattern: parallel `agents.list` / `sessions.list` / `models.list` / `channels.status` RPCs on connect
- 42 unit tests

**Supporting files**: .desktop, .metainfo.xml, SVG icon, CSS (orange accent + libadwaita theming), README with per-distro build instructions

## Known gaps

- **Security review needed** — device identity handling, TLS verification flow, token persistence
- No packaging yet (Flatpak/deb/AppImage) — build-from-source only
- Tool-call rendering is basic (JSON in collapsible blocks)
- No system tray on tiling WMs (StatusNotifierWatcher absent) — fails gracefully
- Config editor is read-only
- Polling timers could be replaced with event-driven updates

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 66 tests pass (24 gateway-client + 42 app)
- [x] Smoke-tested against gateway v2026.4.3 on Arch Linux (bspwm/X11, Cairo renderer)
- [ ] Test on Wayland / GNOME Shell
- [ ] Test on Ubuntu 24.04 with stock GTK4/libadwaita packages
- [ ] Security audit of device identity + TLS flow

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
target.author: 00xglitch

target.title: docs(memory): add memory system deep dive guide
target.body:
## Summary

Add a comprehensive memory system deep dive guide, addressing #43032.

### Problem

`docs/concepts/memory.md` covers basics (~600 words) but lacks depth on memory best practices, search optimization, lifecycle management, and advanced patterns.

### New document: `docs/concepts/memory-deep-dive.md` (401 lines)

Covers all 6 sections requested in the issue:

1. **Memory Architecture** — Two-layer model (`MEMORY.md` + `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`), indexing scope, what is searchable vs not
2. **Writing Effective Memory** — What belongs in MEMORY.md vs daily logs, promotion workflow, formatting tips for better search indexing
3. **Search Optimization** — Hybrid retrieval pipeline (keyword + vector + MMR), query strategies (semantic vs exact), `memory_search` → `memory_get` workflow, tuning parameters (`maxResults`, `minScore`)
4. **Memory Maintenance** — Heartbeat-based periodic review, cleanup strategies, `heartbeat-state.json` tracking
5. **Advanced Patterns** — Entity tracking with stable headings, opinion evolution with timestamps, cross-session recall via `sessionMemory`
6. **Troubleshooting** — No results, keyword-only matches, `memory_get` file restrictions, MEMORY.md bloat, missing transcript search

### Navigation

Added to `docs.json` under Sessions and memory → Memory group, right after `concepts/memory`.

### Source references

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts` — default settings (maxResults: 6, maxSnippetChars: 700)
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/read-file.ts` — `memory_get` file boundary enforcement
- `src/agents/memory-search.ts` — search config resolution and sources
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md` — existing builtin engine docs

Closes #43032

target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (local contributor checkout)
- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm
- Model/provider: parser coverage for OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output; Claude path rechecked via existing tests
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for unit verification
- Relevant config (redacted): CLI backend `output: "jsonl"`

### Steps

1. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary events plus a terminal `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.
- Claude JSONL result handling still passes unchanged.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: existing Claude stream-json tests still pass; `task_complete` outranks `final_answer`; `final_answer` outranks commentary; commentary `item.text` is ignored.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK" 1> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.jsonl 2> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.stderr.txt

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports parseCliJsonl() and createCliJsonlStreamingParser() from ./src/agents/cli-output.ts
feeds the saved Codex CLI JSONL transcript through both parser paths

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports the same parser entry points and replays redacted Codex/OpenAI terminal-field JSONL containing commentary, final_answer, and task_complete records
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}
{"type":"turn.completed","usage":{"input_tokens":22858,"cached_input_tokens":2432,"output_tokens":129,"reasoning_output_tokens":116}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}

```

For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary
Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.
## What it does
**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.
**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS
## Why
Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.
This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`
No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.
## Changes
New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests
Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)
## Test plan
- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅
Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)
---
## Real behavior proof
_Refreshed for current head `f90eee0a` (after the Windows case-insensitive OS-home redaction commit)._

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Doctor Node runtime note renders the executable path via a two-pass redaction (`shortenHomePath` for the OpenClaw home, then an OS-home pass). The OS-home pass compared the Windows home prefix with exact casing, so a valid Windows path whose drive/username casing differed from `os.homedir()` (e.g. `os.homedir()` = `C:\Users\Alice`, `execPath` = `c:\users\alice\...`) fell through unredacted and leaked the OS username. POSIX comparison stays case-sensitive; the Windows backslash-boundary prefix is now compared case-insensitively, slicing by the original length to preserve real casing.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head `f90eee0a`. Invoked the real `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics()` + `buildNodeRuntimeSummary()` — the same render `noteNodeRuntime()` emits in the Doctor health flow — plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered render path via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix (current head):** Block 1 is the real render on this machine. Block 2 is the POSIX OS-home redaction scenario (OPENCLAW_HOME elsewhere). Block 3 is the new Windows case-insensitive redaction this commit fixes. Remaining blocks are injected lifecycle scenarios against the current date.
```
=== 1. Real current-head render (Node v24.14.0) ===
Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install

=== 2. POSIX OS-home redaction (OPENCLAW_HOME=/srv/openclaw, Node under the user's OS home) ===
os home (HOME)     : /home/<user>
input  execPath    : /home/<user>/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== 3. Windows case-insensitive redaction (the new fix) ===
os.homedir()       : C:\Users\Alice   (canonical casing)
input  execPath    : c:\users\alice\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe   (lower-cased)
output rendered    : Node 24.14.0 · ~\AppData\Roaming\nvm\v24.14.0\node.exe · via nvm
leaks OS username? : NO (redacted)

=== Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance) ===
Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~10 months remaining).
Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

=== Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum) ===
Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean note (supported, system install). Both the POSIX OS-home scenario (block 2) and the Windows case-mismatch scenario (block 3) now redact the home to `~`, so no OS username appears — including when `execPath` casing differs from `os.homedir()`. Before this commit, the exact-case Windows prefix check left such paths unredacted. Injected Node 22 LTS / 20 produce the expected maintenance / below-minimum advisories. POSIX, Windows OPENCLAW_HOME-override, and the new Windows case-mismatch regression tests cover the redaction paths; 51 unit tests pass, types and lint clean.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); the registered render path is invoked directly instead. Redaction relies on `os.homedir()`; in the rare case where `HOME`/`USERPROFILE` is unset and differs from the path's owner (e.g. some sudo/container setups), redaction may not apply — out of scope for this detect-only diagnostic.

target.title: feat(irc): support markdown messages via draft/multiline
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for sending markdown-formatted messages over IRC using the `draft/multiline` CAP extension. This preserves newlines in bot responses, allowing IRC clients to properly render markdown content.

## Motivation

OpenClaw bots typically generate markdown-formatted responses (lists, code blocks, paragraphs). Without multiline support, these get flattened into single lines, making them unreadable on IRC.

The `draft/multiline` spec (https://ircv3.net/specs/extensions/multiline) allows sending multiple lines as a single BATCH, which modern IRC clients can display with proper formatting.

## Changes

- **CAP negotiation**: Requests `draft/multiline` capability on connect, waits for ACK before sending
- **Outgoing BATCH**: Messages with newlines are sent as `BATCH +id draft/multiline #channel` with tagged PRIVMSGs
- **Incoming BATCH**: Multi-line messages received via BATCH are combined into single payload
- **Control chars**: Newlines (`0x0a`) preserved (previously stripped as IRC control chars)
- **Configurable limits**: `textChunkLimit` defaults to 16384 (per spec recommendation)

## Compatibility

- **Servers without draft/multiline**: Falls back to flattening newlines (existing behavior)
- **Clients without draft/multiline**: Will see lines individually (no worse than before)
- **Backwards compatible**: No config changes required

## Testing

Tested on Ergo IRC server with `draft/multiline` support. Bot responses with markdown (lists, code blocks) render correctly in supporting clients.

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as “disable switchable session history”.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target session’s stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: feat: allow before-agent-run prompt transforms
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Plugins can currently allow or block `before_agent_run`, but they cannot safely rewrite the prompt that is sent to the model. That makes redaction-style plugins choose between blocking the run or letting sensitive input reach model-bound surfaces.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a `transform` hook decision for `before_agent_run`. A plugin can now return `{ outcome: "transform", prompt }` to replace the prompt at model-bound surfaces while preserving the local transcript prompt.

The implementation routes the transformed prompt through the CLI and embedded model boundaries, `llm_input`, prompt/image handling, trajectory events, final prompt metadata, and context diagnostics. Hook merging keeps `block` as the strongest decision, then `transform`, then `pass`.

## User Impact

Users can install plugins that redact sensitive information before a prompt reaches model providers. Local transcript/history behavior remains unchanged, so users still see the prompt they originally submitted.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/hook-decision-types.test.ts src/plugins/hook-lifecycle-gates.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

### Real-run proof — `before_agent_run` transform

Ran a real `openclaw agent --local` turn on this PR branch with a minimal local plugin that returns `{ outcome: "transform", prompt }` from `before_agent_run` and also registers an `llm_input` observer. Provider: LiteLLM (`openai-completions`) with a real `status=200` model response. All runtime state isolated via `OPENCLAW_HOME`.

**Input prompt:** `My project codename is CONFIDENTIAL-ABC123. Please acknowledge and remember it.`

Plugin + model boundary (stderr):

```
[transform-proof] before_agent_run original    = "My project codename is CONFIDENTIAL-ABC123. Please acknowledge and remember it."
[transform-proof] before_agent_run replacement = "My project codename is [REDACTED-SECRET]. Please acknowledge and remember it."
[transform-proof] llm_input.prompt (model-bound) = "...My project codename is [REDACTED-SECRET]. Please acknowledge and remember it."
[provider-transport-fetch] [model-fetch] response provider=litellm api=openai-completions model=... status=200
```

Model reply (the model received the transformed prompt and echoed it back):

```
Acknowledged. I'll remember that your project codename is [REDACTED-SECRET].
```

Stored canonical transcript (`agents/default/sessions/<id>.jsonl`):

```json
{ "role": "user", "content": [{ "type": "text",
  "text": "My project codename is CONFIDENTIAL-ABC123. Please acknowledge and remember it." }] }
```

The transformed prompt (`[REDACTED-SECRET]`) is what the `llm_input` observer, the outbound model request, and the model's own reply carry. The stored canonical transcript keeps the original prompt verbatim — `[REDACTED-SECRET]` never appears in the transcript jsonl (only in the model-bound path and the diagnostic trajectory). This confirms `transform` replaces the model-bound prompt without rewriting stored transcript text, matching the documented contract.
